documentTitle: Event-Driven Architecture: Reacting to What Happened, Not Being Told What to Do
h1Prefix: Services that never call each other directly,
lede: Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) flips how services talk to each other: instead of one service calling another directly and waiting for a reply, a service simply announces 'something just happened' (an event) — and whoever cares can react on their own, whenever they're ready, without the announcer ever needing to know who's listening. Try the four scenarios below — the basic publish/subscribe pattern, how an event queue absorbs sudden load spikes, two ways to coordinate a workflow across services, and why this architecture is far more resilient when one part of the system is having a bad day.

pubsub.title: OrderService works alone — nobody's listening yet
pubsub.desc: OrderService just finished processing an order. In a world without events, if any other service needs to know about it (send an email, update stock, log a metric), OrderService would have to call each of those services directly, one by one — and know exactly who to call.

pubsub.title: Three services register as subscribers — without knowing each other
pubsub.desc: EmailService, InventoryService, and AnalyticsService each register their interest in one event type, say "OrderPlaced", with the Event Bus. None of them know anything about the other two — and importantly, OrderService doesn't even know they exist.

pubsub.title: OrderService publishes an OrderPlaced event to the Event Bus
pubsub.desc: OrderService just announces one thing: 'an order was just placed', along with its data. It sends this to the Event Bus and immediately moves on to other work — not caring, or even knowing, who (if anyone) will react to this event.
fields: the Event Bus (not any specific service)

pubsub.title: The Event Bus delivers a copy of the event to ALL subscribers at once
pubsub.desc: The Event Bus forwards one copy of the OrderPlaced event to every service that registered earlier — EmailService, InventoryService, and AnalyticsService all receive it simultaneously and independently of one another.

pubsub.title: Each subscriber processes the event in its own way
pubsub.desc: EmailService sends a confirmation email, InventoryService decrements stock, AnalyticsService logs a metric — all three run at their own pace, without waiting on each other, and without blocking OrderService, which finished long ago and moved on to other work.

pubsub.title: A new subscriber can be added without touching OrderService's code at all
pubsub.desc: Say the security team wants to add a FraudDetectionService to flag suspicious orders. They just register it as a new subscriber for the OrderPlaced event — OrderService, which publishes that event, doesn't need to be touched, or even redeployed, at all.
fields: none — this is the whole point of decoupling

queue.title: A producer, an empty queue, and a consumer ready to work
queue.desc: PaymentService (the producer) is about to send events into a queue (a message broker), and NotificationService (the consumer) will pull from it to process them. Right now the queue is empty — nothing has been sent yet.

queue.title: The producer sends 5 events in quick succession
queue.desc: PaymentService processes five payment transactions in a short span of time, and sends an event into the queue as soon as each one completes. The producer doesn't wait for the queue to be 'empty' first — it just drops the event in and moves on to the next thing.

queue.tag: CONSUMER PROCESSES ONE AT A TIME
queue.title: The consumer pulls events off the front of the queue, one by one
queue.desc: NotificationService pulls the oldest event (FIFO), fully processes it, then pulls the next one. The consumer's speed can be wildly different from the producer's — the queue absorbs that difference, instead of forcing the producer to wait.
fields: shrinking gradually as items are processed

queue.title: NotificationService crashes mid-way — but no event is lost
queue.desc: The consumer suddenly stops (say, it's being redeployed, or it crashed). Any events that hadn't been processed yet stay safely sitting in the queue — nothing just disappears. PaymentService doesn't even notice anything's wrong, because it never waits on the consumer to respond in the first place.
fields: still safely stored in the queue, not lost

queue.title: NotificationService comes back online and resumes where it left off
queue.desc: Once the consumer is back, it immediately resumes pulling events from the queue exactly where it stopped — catching up on everything that piled up while it was down, with nothing needing to be manually resent.
fields: drains the backlog of events, one at a time

queue.title: The queue acted as a shock absorber between two different speeds
queue.desc: All the events eventually get processed. The queue acted as a buffer, protecting the producer from ever having to wait on the consumer, and protecting the consumer from being overwhelmed by sudden spikes — and it stayed safe even through a brief outage on either side.

choreography.title: One business process, three services that must run in order
choreography.desc: When an order is placed, three things need to happen in sequence: OrderService records the order, PaymentService charges the payment, then ShippingService ships the goods. The question is: who's in charge of making sure this order happens?

choreography.title: There are two common ways to coordinate a flow like this
choreography.desc: Choreography: no central coordinator, each service reacts on its own to events from other services. Orchestration: one central coordinator explicitly tells each service when to do its job.
fields: each service reacts independently to events
one central coordinator drives every step

choreography.title: Each service reacts to the previous service's event, independently
choreography.desc: OrderService publishes an "OrderPlaced" event. PaymentService, which subscribes to that event, automatically processes it and then publishes its own "PaymentCompleted" event. ShippingService, which subscribes to "PaymentCompleted", automatically starts shipping. No single party knows the full flow — everyone is just reacting to whatever event comes their way.

choreography.title: A single Orchestrator explicitly calls each service in sequence
choreography.desc: For comparison: with orchestration, there's one extra service — an Orchestrator — that actively calls OrderService, waits for it to finish, then calls PaymentService, waits, then calls ShippingService. This Orchestrator knows and drives the entire flow from a single place.

choreography.title: The trade-offs run in opposite directions
choreography.desc: Choreography: no central point that can become a bottleneck, easy to add new reactions without touching other services — but the overall flow becomes hard to see all at once, scattered across many services. Orchestration: the flow is easy to monitor and trace from a single place — but the Orchestrator becomes a single point of failure and can itself become a bottleneck.
fields: no single point of failure, easy to extend
the overall flow is scattered, hard to trace
the Orchestrator becomes a single point of failure

choreography.title: Neither one is 'more correct' — it depends on the need
choreography.desc: Small teams with simple flows often prefer choreography for its flexibility and ease of extension. Long, complex business processes that need full visibility (say, needing to know exactly 'what stage is this order at') tend to fit orchestration better.
fields: simple flows, small teams, need for flexibility
complex flows that need visibility & centralized control

resilience.title: The synchronous version: OrderService calls directly and waits
resilience.desc: In a synchronous design (say, over plain REST APIs), OrderService calls PaymentService directly and WAITS for a reply, and PaymentService in turn calls ShippingService directly and waits for its reply too. Everything is chained together, each link waiting on the next.
fields: blocking — every caller waits for a response

resilience.title: When everything's healthy, the chain completes — but the customer waits for all of it
resilience.desc: When all three services are healthy, this call chain eventually completes and the customer gets a success response. But notice: the customer has to wait for the ENTIRE chain to finish — total wait time is the sum of all three services, not just OrderService alone.
fields: the sum of all three services

resilience.title: ShippingService goes down → the entire chain fails with it
resilience.desc: Now ShippingService is having trouble and isn't responding. PaymentService's call to ShippingService fails or times out — which makes PaymentService's own call fail — which in turn makes OrderService's call fail too. The customer sees an error message, EVEN THOUGH the payment may already have gone through before the failure happened.
fields: the failure cascades up to Payment, then to Order
an error message, even though payment may already be recorded

resilience.title: OrderService just publishes an event and it's done
resilience.desc: Compare that to the event-driven version: OrderService just publishes an "OrderPlaced" event and IMMEDIATELY tells the customer their order was received — without waiting on PaymentService or ShippingService at all.
fields: publishes an event, then it's done
only OrderService, not the whole chain

resilience.title: ShippingService is down — but nothing else fails with it
resilience.desc: ShippingService is still having trouble. The difference: the shipping event just sits safely in the queue until ShippingService recovers — PaymentService keeps running normally, and the customer ALREADY got their order-received confirmation long before this problem even happened. No error is visible to the customer at all.
fields: the event waits in the queue — Payment & the customer response are unaffected

resilience.title: Resilient, at a price: nothing happens all at once anymore
resilience.desc: Event-driven is far more resilient to partial failures and feels faster to the customer. The price: eventual consistency — the shipment might only actually get processed a little while later, not instantly, and tracing 'what stage is this order at' takes more effort than a synchronous chain, which is easy to trace step by step.
fields: event-driven is far more resilient to partial failures
eventual consistency — the final result doesn't happen instantly
